annotations.label: Probability of an event (0 to 1)

anchor.snippet: The probability of an event is a number between 0 and 1

annotations.note: `IsProbabilityMeasure.measure_univ` plus `prob_le_one` encode that a probability measure assigns values in [0,1].

annotations.label: Fair coin toss

anchor.snippet: A simple example is the tossing of a fair (unbiased) coin

annotations.note: No dedicated fair-coin example was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Theoretical probability

anchor.snippet: probabilities can be numerically described by the number of desired outcomes, divided by the total number of all outcomes

annotations.note: The classical favourable/total-outcomes definition is captured by `uniformOn` (the uniform/counting probability measure on a finite set).

annotations.label: Two coin tosses outcomes

anchor.snippet: tossing a coin twice will yield

annotations.note: No explicit example of the four outcomes of two coin tosses found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Frequentist probability

anchor.snippet: frequentist probability , which claims that the probability of a random event denotes the relative frequency of occurrence

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the strong law of large numbers (which justifies the frequentist limit) but not the philosophical interpretation itself.

annotations.label: Propensity probability

anchor.snippet: propensity probability , which interprets probability as the tendency of some experiment to yield a certain outcome

annotations.note: Propensity is a philosophical interpretation with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Subjective (Bayesian) probability

anchor.snippet: Bayesian probability , which includes expert knowledge as well as experimental data to produce probabilities

annotations.note: The Bayesian interpretation as such is not formalized; only Bayes' theorem is.

annotations.label: Aumann's agreement theorem

anchor.snippet: By Aumann's agreement theorem , Bayesian agents whose prior beliefs are similar will end up with similar posterior beliefs

annotations.note: Aumann's agreement theorem is only listed in docs/1000.yaml as an unformalized goal.

annotations.label: Cardano's odds as ratio

anchor.snippet: defining odds as the ratio of favourable to unfavourable outcomes

annotations.note: No `odds` definition as favourable-to-unfavourable ratio found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Laplace's first law of error

anchor.snippet: the frequency of an error could be expressed as an exponential function of the numerical magnitude of the error

annotations.note: Laplace's first error law (Laplace/double-exponential distribution) is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Laplace's second law of error (normal distribution)

anchor.snippet: the frequency of the error is an exponential function of the square of the error

annotations.note: The normal distribution is formalized as `gaussianReal` with PDF `gaussianPDFReal`.

annotations.label: Sample space

anchor.snippet: The collection of all possible results is called the sample space of the experiment

annotations.note: The sample space is the underlying type of a `MeasureSpace`/`MeasurableSpace` carrying the probability measure.

annotations.label: Event as subset of power set

anchor.snippet: These collections are called "events"

annotations.note: Events are formalized as `MeasurableSet`s in the σ-algebra on the sample space.

annotations.label: Odd-number event on a die

anchor.snippet: rolling a die can produce six possible results

annotations.note: No die-roll example for the odd-number event found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probability assignment axioms

anchor.snippet: A probability is a way of assigning every event a value between zero and one

annotations.note: Kolmogorov-style probability axioms are captured by `Measure` together with `IsProbabilityMeasure` (μ univ = 1).

annotations.label: Notation for probability of event A

anchor.snippet: The probability of an event A is written as

annotations.note: Probability of an event A is written `μ A` for a `Measure μ`.

annotations.label: Complement of an event

anchor.snippet: The opposite or complement of an event A is the event

annotations.note: `prob_add_prob_compl` states `μ s + μ sᶜ = 1` for a probability measure.

annotations.label: Chance of not rolling a six

anchor.snippet: the chance of not rolling a six on a six-sided die

annotations.note: No specific die-roll example for P(not 6) = 5/6 found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Joint probability (intersection)

anchor.snippet: this is called the intersection or joint probability of A and B

annotations.note: Joint probability is `μ (A ∩ B)`, directly expressible via the measure of the intersection.

annotations.label: Joint probability for independent events

anchor.snippet: If two events, A and B are independent then the joint probability is

annotations.note: `indepSet_iff_measure_inter_eq_mul` characterizes independence by μ(A ∩ B) = μ A · μ B.

annotations.label: Two coins both heads

anchor.snippet: if two coins are flipped, then the chance of both being heads is

annotations.note: No two-coins-both-heads worked example in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mutually exclusive events

anchor.snippet: If either event A or event B can occur but never both simultaneously, then they are called mutually exclusive events

annotations.note: Mutually exclusive events correspond to `Disjoint s t` (i.e., s ∩ t = ∅).

annotations.label: Probability rules for mutually exclusive events

anchor.snippet: If two events are mutually exclusive , then the probability of both occurring is denoted as

annotations.note: `measure_union` gives μ(A ∪ B) = μ A + μ B when A and B are disjoint (and μ(A ∩ B) = 0).

anchors.snippet: If two events are mutually exclusive , then the probability of both occurring is denoted as

annotations.label: Rolling 1 or 2 on a die

anchor.snippet: the chance of rolling a 1 or 2 on a six-sided die

annotations.note: No worked die example computing P(1 or 2) = 1/3 found.

annotations.label: Inclusion–exclusion for two events

anchor.snippet: If the events are not (necessarily) mutually exclusive then

annotations.note: `measure_union_add_inter` gives μ(A ∪ B) + μ(A ∩ B) = μ A + μ B, equivalent to the two-event inclusion–exclusion.

anchors.snippet: If the events are not (necessarily) mutually exclusive then

annotations.label: Heart or face card from a deck

anchor.snippet: when drawing a card from a deck of cards, the chance of getting a heart or a face card

annotations.note: No card-deck example for P(heart ∪ face) found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inclusion–exclusion for three events

anchor.snippet: This can be expanded further for multiple not (necessarily) mutually exclusive events

annotations.note: General finite inclusion–exclusion for measures is available in Mathlib, specializing to three events.

anchors.snippet: This can be expanded further for multiple not (necessarily) mutually exclusive events

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}P\left(A\cup B\cup C\right)=&P\left(\left(A\cup B\right)\cup C\right)\\=&P\left(A\cup B\right)+P\left(C\right)-P\left(\left(A\cup B\right)\cap C\right)\\=&P\left(A\right)+P\left(B\right)-P\left(A\cap B\right)+P\left(C\right)-P\left(\left(A\cap C\right)\cup \left(B\cap C\right)\right)\\=&P\left(A\right)+P\left(B\right)+P\left(C\right)-P\left(A\cap B\right)-\left(P\left(A\cap C\right)+P\left(B\cap C\right)-P\left(\left(A\cap C\right)\cap \left(B\cap C\right)\right)\right)\\P\left(A\cup B\cup C\right)=&P\left(A\right)+P\left(B\right)+P\left(C\right)-P\left(A\cap B\right)-P\left(A\cap C\right)-P\left(B\cap C\right)+P\left(A\cap B\cap C\right)\end{aligned}}}

annotations.label: Conditional probability

anchor.snippet: Conditional probability is the probability of some event A , given the occurrence of some other event B

annotations.note: `cond` defines the conditional probability measure `μ[· | s] = (μ s)⁻¹ • μ.restrict s`.

annotations.label: Drawing red/blue balls from a bag

anchor.snippet: in a bag of 2 red balls and 2 blue balls

annotations.note: No coloured-balls-in-a-bag example for conditional probability found.

annotations.label: Bayes' rule (posterior proportional to prior times likelihood)

anchor.snippet: Bayes' rule relates the odds of event

annotations.note: `cond_eq_inv_mul_cond_mul` is labelled as Bayes' Theorem: μ[t|s] = (μ s)⁻¹ · μ[s|t] · μ t.
